title: Das Mädchen ohne Hände
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 31. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The thirty-first tale in the Grimms’ collection (KHM 31), one of the darkest in the book. An impoverished miller unwittingly promises the Devil ‘what stands behind the mill’ — his own daughter — and when her purity thwarts the Evil One, the father is driven to chop off her hands; she wanders, is taken to wife by a king who gives her silver hands, and while he is at war the Devil forges letters that have her and her child cast out, until under an angel’s care in a forest hut her hands grow back and she is at last reunited with the king. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as daß, Armuth, Reichthum, thun/that/thust, tödten, wüthend, gieng/fieng, ward, erschrack and the ß in Bißen and Schleuße.

sentences.t: Ein Müller war nach und nach in Armuth gerathen und hatte nichts mehr als seine Mühle und einen großen Apfelbaum dahinter.
sentences.l: A miller had little by little fallen into poverty, and had nothing more than his mill and a great apple-tree behind it.
sentences.i: A miller had gradually fallen into poverty, and had nothing left but his mill and a great apple-tree behind it.
sentences.n: “Armuth” and “gerathen” carry the 1857 -th- (modern Armut, geraten). “nach und nach” is a set phrase, ‘little by little, gradually.’

sentences.t: Einmal war er in den Wald gegangen Holz zu holen, da trat ein alter Mann zu ihm, den er noch niemals gesehen hatte, und sprach „was quälst du dich mit Holzhacken, ich will dich reich machen, wenn du mir versprichst was hinter deiner Mühle steht.“
sentences.l: Once he had gone into the wood to fetch wood; there an old man stepped up to him, whom he had never yet seen, and spoke, “why do you plague yourself with wood-chopping? I will make you rich if you promise me what stands behind your mill.”
sentences.i: One day he had gone into the forest to fetch wood, when an old man he had never seen before stepped up to him and said, “Why do you torment yourself with chopping wood? I will make you rich, if you promise me what stands behind your mill.”
sentences.n: This is the first of the tale’s rash-promise / devil’s-bargain moments: the stranger asks for “what stands behind your mill” without naming it — the whole plot turns on the miller not knowing what he is promising. “quälst du dich” — reflexive sich quälen, ‘to plague / torment oneself.’

sentences.t: „Was kann das anders sein als mein Apfelbaum?“ dachte der Müller, sagte „ja,“ und verschrieb es dem fremden Manne.
sentences.l: “What can that be other than my apple-tree?” thought the miller, said “yes,” and signed it over to the strange man.
sentences.i: “What could that be but my apple-tree?” thought the miller, said “yes,” and made it over to the stranger.
sentences.n: “verschrieb” (verschreiben) is to make over by written contract, ‘to sign away’ — a legal word that quietly seals the bargain. “dem fremden Manne” is dative with the old -e ending.

sentences.t: Der aber lachte höhnisch und sagte „nach drei Jahren will ich kommen und abholen was mir gehört,“ und gieng fort.
sentences.l: But he laughed scornfully and said, “after three years I will come and carry off what belongs to me,” and went away.
sentences.i: But the man laughed scornfully and said, “In three years I will come and fetch what belongs to me,” and went away.
sentences.n: “gieng” is the 1857 spelling of ging (‘went’); this -ie- spelling of gehen recurs throughout (gieng, fieng, anfieng). “gehört mir” — gehören takes the dative, ‘belongs to me.’

sentences.t: Als der Müller nach Haus kam, trat ihm seine Frau entgegen und sprach „sage mir, Müller, woher kommt der plötzliche Reichthum in unser Haus? auf einmal sind alle Kisten und Kasten voll, kein Mensch hats hereingebracht, und ich weiß nicht wie es zugegangen ist.“
sentences.l: When the miller came home, his wife stepped toward him and spoke, “tell me, miller, whence comes the sudden wealth into our house? all at once all chests and boxes are full, no one has brought it in, and I do not know how it came about.”
sentences.i: When the miller came home, his wife came to meet him and said, “Tell me, miller, where does this sudden wealth in our house come from? All at once every chest and box is full, no one brought it in, and I don’t know how it happened.”
sentences.n: “trat ihm entgegen” — entgegentreten (‘to step toward, meet’) takes the dative ihm. “hats” is a colloquial contraction of hat es. “wie es zugegangen ist” — zugehen here means ‘to come about, happen.’ “Reichthum” has the old -th-.

sentences.t: Er antwortete, „das kommt von einem fremden Manne, der mir im Walde begegnet ist und mir große Schätze verheißen hat; ich habe ihm dagegen verschrieben was hinter der Mühle steht: den großen Apfelbaum können wir wohl dafür geben.“
sentences.l: He answered, “that comes from a strange man who met me in the wood and promised me great treasures; I have made over to him in return what stands behind the mill: the great apple-tree we can well give for it.”
sentences.i: He answered, “That comes from a stranger who met me in the forest and promised me great riches; in return I made over to him what stands behind the mill — the great apple-tree we can surely give for that.”
sentences.n: “dagegen” here means ‘in return, in exchange.’ “verheißen” is an elevated word for ‘to promise, foretell.’ “begegnen” takes the dative (mir).

sentences.t: „Ach, Mann,“ sagte die Frau erschrocken, „das ist der Teufel gewesen: den Apfelbaum hat er nicht gemeint, sondern unsere Tochter, die stand hinter der Mühle und kehrte den Hof.“
sentences.l: “Oh, husband,” said the wife, frightened, “that was the Devil: the apple-tree he did not mean, but our daughter, who stood behind the mill and swept the yard.”
sentences.i: “Oh, husband,” said the wife, terrified, “that was the Devil! It was not the apple-tree he meant, but our daughter, who was standing behind the mill sweeping the yard.”
sentences.n: The bargain’s trap springs shut: “what stands behind the mill” was the daughter all along. “nicht ... sondern” = ‘not ... but (rather).’ “kehren” here is ‘to sweep’ (den Hof kehren, to sweep the yard).

sentences.t: Die Müllerstochter war ein schönes und frommes Mädchen, und lebte die drei Jahre in Gottesfurcht und ohne Sünde.
sentences.l: The miller’s daughter was a beautiful and pious girl, and lived the three years in fear of God and without sin.
sentences.n: “Gottesfurcht” (fear of God, godliness) and “ohne Sünde” (without sin) set up the religious frame of the whole tale: it is her purity that will keep the Devil at bay. “fromm” = pious, devout — a key word here.

sentences.t: Als nun die Zeit herum war, und der Tag kam, wo sie der Böse holen wollte, da wusch sie sich rein und machte mit Kreide einen Kranz um sich.
sentences.l: When now the time was up and the day came on which the Evil One wanted to fetch her, she washed herself clean and made with chalk a ring around herself.
sentences.i: Now when the time was up and the day came on which the Evil One meant to fetch her, she washed herself clean and drew a ring of chalk around herself.
sentences.n: “der Böse” — literally ‘the Evil One,’ a common German name for the Devil. “die Zeit war herum” = the time was up / over. The chalk “Kranz” (ring, circle) is a protective magic circle.

sentences.t: Der Teufel erschien ganz frühe, aber er konnte ihr nicht nahe kommen.
sentences.l: The Devil appeared quite early, but he could not come near to her.
sentences.n: “nahe kommen” + dative (ihr) = ‘to come near to her’; her purity keeps him at a distance.

sentences.t: Zornig sprach er zum Müller „thu ihr alles Wasser weg, damit sie sich nicht mehr waschen kann, denn sonst habe ich keine Gewalt über sie.“
sentences.l: Angrily he spoke to the miller, “take all the water away from her, so that she can no longer wash herself, for otherwise I have no power over her.”
sentences.i: Angrily he said to the miller, “Take all her water away, so she can wash herself no more, for otherwise I have no power over her.”
sentences.n: “thu ... weg” is the 1857 spelling of tu ... weg (wegtun, ‘to take away’); thun/thu carries the old -th- (modern tun). “Gewalt über” = power / dominion over.

sentences.t: Der Müller fürchtete sich und that es.
sentences.l: The miller was afraid and did it.
sentences.n: “that” is the 1857 spelling of tat (thun = modern tun, ‘to do’); you will meet this -th- throughout the edition.

sentences.t: Am andern Morgen kam der Teufel wieder, aber sie hatte auf ihre Hände geweint, und sie waren ganz rein.
sentences.l: The next morning the Devil came again, but she had wept on her hands, and they were quite clean.
sentences.i: The next morning the Devil came again, but she had wept upon her hands, and they were perfectly clean.
sentences.n: “am andern Morgen” = the next morning (andern is the old spelling of anderen). Her tears take the place of the water — piety keeps outwitting the Devil.

sentences.t: Da konnte er ihr wiederum nicht nahen und sprach wüthend zu dem Müller „hau ihr die Hände ab, sonst kann ich ihr nichts anhaben.“
sentences.l: Then again he could not draw near to her, and spoke furiously to the miller, “chop off her hands, otherwise I can do nothing to her.”
sentences.i: Then once more he could not come near her, and said furiously to the miller, “Chop off her hands, or else I can do nothing to her.”
sentences.n: “wüthend” is the 1857 spelling of wütend (‘furious’), with the old -th-. “hau ... ab” (abhauen, ‘to chop / hew off’) is separable — the prefix ab goes to the end. “jemandem nichts anhaben können” is an idiom, ‘to be unable to harm someone.’ The mutilation motif proper begins here.

sentences.t: Der Müller entsetzte sich und antwortete „wie könnt ich meinem eigenen Kinde die Hände abhauen!“
sentences.l: The miller was horrified and answered, “how could I chop off the hands of my own child!”
sentences.i: The miller was aghast and answered, “How could I chop off my own child’s hands!”
sentences.n: “entsetzte sich” — reflexive sich entsetzen, ‘to be horrified, aghast.’ “meinem eigenen Kinde” is dative (with old -e); German uses the dative for the person whose hands are chopped, ‘chop the hands off my child.’

sentences.t: Da drohte ihm der Böse und sprach „wo du es nicht thust, so bist du mein, und ich hole dich selber.“
sentences.l: Then the Evil One threatened him and spoke, “where you do not do it, then you are mine, and I fetch you myself.”
sentences.i: Then the Evil One threatened him and said, “If you do not do it, you are mine, and I will come for you myself.”
sentences.n: “wo” here has the old conditional sense ‘if’ (= wenn). “thust” is the 1857 spelling of tust. The bargain tightens: the father must maim his daughter or be taken himself.

sentences.t: Dem Vater ward angst, und er versprach ihm zu gehorchen.
sentences.l: To the father fear came, and he promised to obey him.
sentences.i: The father grew afraid, and promised to obey him.
sentences.n: “ward” is the archaic preterite of werden (‘became / was’); modern German uses wurde — you will meet ward constantly. “mir wird angst” is an impersonal dative idiom, ‘I grow afraid.’ “gehorchen” takes the dative (ihm).

sentences.t: Da gieng er zu dem Mädchen und sagte „mein Kind, wenn ich dir nicht beide Hände abhaue, so führt mich der Teufel fort, und in der Angst hab ich es ihm versprochen. Hilf mir doch in meiner Noth und verzeihe mir was ich böses an dir thue.“
sentences.l: Then he went to the girl and said, “my child, if I do not chop off both your hands, then the Devil carries me away, and in my fear I promised it to him. Do help me in my need and forgive me the wrong I do to you.”
sentences.i: Then he went to the girl and said, “My child, if I do not chop off both your hands, the Devil will carry me off — in my terror I promised it to him. Help me, then, in my distress, and forgive me the wrong I do you.”
sentences.n: “abhaue” is the 1st sg. present of abhauen. “führt ... fort” (fortführen, ‘to carry off’) is separable. “Noth” is the 1857 spelling of Not (‘need, distress’), with the old -th-. “verzeihe mir” (verzeihen, ‘to forgive’) takes the dative person.

sentences.t: Sie antwortete, „lieber Vater, macht mit mir was ihr wollt, ich bin euer Kind.“
sentences.l: She answered, “dear father, do with me what you will, I am your child.”
sentences.i: She answered, “Dear father, do with me whatever you will; I am your child.”
sentences.n: The daughter addresses her father with the respectful plural “ihr / euer” (‘you / your’) — old-fashioned deference of a child to a parent, kept throughout her speech.

sentences.t: Darauf legte sie beide Hände hin und ließ sie sich abhauen.
sentences.l: Thereupon she laid both hands out and let them be chopped off from her.
sentences.i: Then she laid out both her hands and let them be chopped off.
sentences.n: “legte ... hin” (hinlegen, ‘to lay out / down’) is separable. “ließ sie sich abhauen” — ‘let them be chopped off (from herself)’; lassen + reflexive expresses letting something be done to oneself. The tale does not soften the horror.

sentences.t: Der Teufel kam zum drittenmal, aber sie hatte so lange und so viel auf die Stümpfe geweint, daß sie doch ganz rein waren.
sentences.l: The Devil came for the third time, but she had wept so long and so much upon the stumps that they were, after all, quite clean.
sentences.i: The Devil came for the third time, but she had wept so long and so hard upon the stumps that they were, in spite of all, perfectly clean.
sentences.n: “Stümpfe” = stumps (of her arms). “daß” is the 1857 spelling of dass (‘that’); the ß is kept throughout this edition. Even mutilated, her tears keep her pure.

sentences.t: Da mußte er weichen und hatte alles Recht auf sie verloren.
sentences.l: Then he had to give way and had lost all right to her.
sentences.i: Then he had to withdraw, and had lost all claim to her.
sentences.n: “mußte” is the 1857 spelling of musste. “weichen” = to yield, give way, retreat. “Recht auf sie” = right / claim to her — the language of the broken bargain.

sentences.t: Der Müller sprach zu ihr „ich habe so großes Gut durch dich gewonnen, ich will dich zeitlebens aufs köstlichste halten.“
sentences.l: The miller spoke to her, “I have gained such great wealth through you, I will keep you all your life in the most sumptuous way.”
sentences.i: The miller said to her, “I have gained such great wealth through you; I will keep you in the greatest comfort all your life.”
sentences.n: “Gut” here means ‘wealth, goods, property.’ “zeitlebens” = for life, all her life. “aufs köstlichste halten” = to keep (someone) in the most exquisite / sumptuous style (aufs + superlative is an adverbial construction).

sentences.t: Sie antwortete aber „hier kann ich nicht bleiben: ich will fortgehen: mitleidige Menschen werden mir schon so viel geben als ich brauche.“
sentences.l: But she answered, “here I cannot stay: I will go away: compassionate people will surely give me as much as I need.”
sentences.i: But she answered, “I cannot stay here; I will go away. Compassionate folk will surely give me as much as I need.”
sentences.n: “mitleidige” = compassionate, pitying. “schon” here is a reassuring particle (‘surely, no doubt’), not ‘already.’ “so viel ... als” = as much as (older than the modern so viel ... wie).

sentences.t: Darauf ließ sie sich die verstümmelten Arme auf den Rücken binden, und mit Sonnenaufgang machte sie sich auf den Weg und gieng den ganzen Tag bis es Nacht ward.
sentences.l: Thereupon she had her mutilated arms bound onto her back, and with sunrise she set out on the way and went the whole day until it became night.
sentences.i: Then she had her maimed arms bound onto her back, and at sunrise she set out and walked all day until night fell.
sentences.n: “verstümmelten” = mutilated, maimed. “sich auf den Weg machen” is an idiom, ‘to set out.’ “ward” again — the archaic preterite of werden, here ‘became.’

sentences.t: Da kam sie zu einem königlichen Garten, und beim Mondschimmer sah sie daß Bäume voll schöner Früchte darin standen; aber sie konnte nicht hinein, denn es war ein Wasser darum.
sentences.l: There she came to a royal garden, and by the moonlight-shimmer she saw that trees full of beautiful fruits stood in it; but she could not get in, for there was a water around it.
sentences.i: There she came to a royal garden, and by the shimmer of the moon she saw that trees full of fine fruit stood within; but she could not get in, for a moat of water lay around it.
sentences.n: “Mondschimmer” = the shimmer / soft light of the moon. “konnte nicht hinein” — the verb of motion is dropped after the modal, ‘could not [go] in.’ The “Wasser” is the moat, named a Graben (ditch) below.

sentences.t: Und weil sie den ganzen Tag gegangen war und keinen Bißen genossen hatte, und der Hunger sie quälte, so dachte sie „ach, wäre ich darin, damit ich etwas von den Früchten äße, sonst muß ich verschmachten.“
sentences.l: And because she had walked the whole day and had partaken of no morsel, and hunger tormented her, she thought, “oh, were I in there, so that I might eat something of the fruits, otherwise I must languish away.”
sentences.i: And because she had walked all day and eaten not a morsel, and hunger tormented her, she thought, “Oh, if only I were inside, that I might eat some of the fruit, or else I must waste away.”
sentences.n: “Bißen” is the 1857 spelling of Bissen (‘morsel, bite’). “genossen” (genießen) here = ‘partaken of, eaten.’ “wäre ... äße” are subjunctive II — the wish ‘were I in there ... that I might eat.’ “verschmachten” = to languish, waste away, pine.

sentences.t: Da kniete sie nieder, rief Gott den Herrn an und betete.
sentences.l: Then she knelt down, called upon God the Lord, and prayed.
sentences.n: “kniete ... nieder” (niederknien, ‘to kneel down’) is separable. “rief ... an” (anrufen, ‘to call upon, invoke’) is separable. Prayer is the tale’s recurring answer to distress — the pious heroine is repeatedly rescued after she prays.

sentences.t: Auf einmal kam ein Engel daher, der machte eine Schleuße in dem Wasser zu, so daß der Graben trocken ward und sie hindurch gehen konnte.
sentences.l: All at once an angel came along, who closed a sluice in the water, so that the ditch became dry and she could go through it.
sentences.i: All at once an angel came, who shut a sluice in the water, so that the moat ran dry and she could pass through.
sentences.n: “Schleuße” is the 1857 spelling of Schleuse (‘sluice, floodgate’). “machte ... zu” (zumachen, ‘to close, shut’) is separable. “Graben” = ditch, moat. The angel is the heroine’s guardian for the rest of the tale.

sentences.t: Nun gieng sie in den Garten, und der Engel gieng mit ihr.
sentences.l: Now she went into the garden, and the angel went with her.

sentences.t: Sie sah einen Baum mit Obst, das waren schöne Birnen, aber sie waren alle gezählt.
sentences.l: She saw a tree with fruit; those were beautiful pears, but they were all counted.
sentences.i: She saw a tree with fruit — they were fine pears, but they had all been counted.
sentences.n: “Obst” = fruit (collective). “alle gezählt” — the pears have all been counted, which is why the king will later notice one missing; a small realistic touch that drives the next scene.

sentences.t: Da trat sie hinzu und aß eine mit dem Munde vom Baume ab, ihren Hunger zu stillen, aber nicht mehr.
sentences.l: Then she stepped up and ate one off the tree with her mouth, to still her hunger, but no more.
sentences.i: Then she stepped up and ate one off the tree with her mouth, to satisfy her hunger, but no more.
sentences.n: “trat ... hinzu” (hinzutreten, ‘to step up to’) is separable. “aß ... ab” (abessen, ‘to eat off’) is separable. Having no hands, she must eat straight from the branch with her mouth. “den Hunger stillen” = to satisfy / still one’s hunger.

sentences.t: Der Gärtner sah es mit an, weil aber der Engel dabei stand, fürchtete er sich und meinte das Mädchen wäre ein Geist, schwieg still und getraute nicht zu rufen oder den Geist anzureden.
sentences.l: The gardener looked on at it, but because the angel stood by, he was afraid and thought the girl was a spirit, kept silent, and did not dare to call out or to address the spirit.
sentences.i: The gardener looked on, but because the angel stood beside her, he was afraid and thought the girl was a ghost; he kept silent, and did not dare to call out or to speak to the ghost.
sentences.n: “sah es mit an” (mit ansehen, ‘to look on at’) is separable. “wäre” is subjunctive II in his reported thought. “Geist” means both ‘spirit’ and ‘ghost’ here. “schwieg still” = kept quite silent. “anzureden” (anreden, ‘to address, speak to’) is separable, here as a zu-infinitive.

sentences.t: Als sie die Birne gegessen hatte, war sie gesättigt, und gieng und versteckte sich in das Gebüsch.
sentences.l: When she had eaten the pear, she was satisfied, and went and hid herself in the bushes.
sentences.i: When she had eaten the pear, she was satisfied, and went and hid herself in the bushes.
sentences.n: “gesättigt” = sated, satisfied. “versteckte sich” — reflexive sich verstecken, ‘to hide oneself.’

sentences.t: Der König, dem der Garten gehörte, kam am andern Morgen herab, da zählte er und sah daß eine der Birnen fehlte, und fragte den Gärtner wo sie hingekommen wäre: sie läge nicht unter dem Baume und wäre doch weg.
sentences.l: The king, to whom the garden belonged, came down the next morning; then he counted and saw that one of the pears was missing, and asked the gardener where it had gone: it lay not under the tree and yet was gone.
sentences.i: The king, to whom the garden belonged, came down the next morning; he counted and saw that one of the pears was missing, and asked the gardener where it had gone — it was not lying under the tree, and yet it was gone.
sentences.n: “gehörte” (gehören + dative) — the garden belonged ‘to’ the king. “hingekommen wäre ... läge ... wäre” are subjunctive of reported speech (the king’s indirect question), pervasive in Grimm.

sentences.t: Da antwortete der Gärtner „vorige Nacht kam ein Geist herein, der hatte keine Hände und aß eine mit dem Munde ab.“
sentences.l: Then the gardener answered, “last night a spirit came in, who had no hands and ate one off with his mouth.”
sentences.i: Then the gardener answered, “Last night a ghost came in; it had no hands, and ate one off the tree with its mouth.”
sentences.n: “vorige Nacht” = last night. “kam ... herein” (hereinkommen) and “aß ... ab” (abessen) are separable.

sentences.t: Der König sprach „wie ist der Geist über das Wasser herein gekommen? und wo ist er hingegangen, nachdem er die Birne gegessen hatte?“
sentences.l: The king spoke, “how did the spirit come in over the water? and where did it go after it had eaten the pear?”
sentences.i: The king said, “How did the ghost get in across the water? And where did it go after it had eaten the pear?”
sentences.n: “herein gekommen” (hereinkommen) and “hingegangen” (hingehen) are separable verbs of motion in the perfect tense.

sentences.t: Der Gärtner antwortete „es kam jemand in schneeweißem Kleide vom Himmel, der hat die Schleuße zugemacht und das Wasser gehemmt, damit der Geist durch den Graben gehen konnte. Und weil es ein Engel muß gewesen sein, so habe ich mich gefürchtet, nicht gefragt und nicht gerufen. Als der Geist die Birne gegessen hatte, ist er wieder zurückgegangen.“
sentences.l: The gardener answered, “someone came in a snow-white garment from heaven, who closed the sluice and stemmed the water, so that the spirit could go through the ditch. And because it must have been an angel, I was afraid, did not ask and did not call. When the spirit had eaten the pear, it went back again.”
sentences.i: The gardener answered, “Someone came from heaven in a snow-white garment, who shut the sluice and stemmed the water, so that the ghost could pass through the moat. And because it must have been an angel, I was afraid, and neither asked nor called out. When the ghost had eaten the pear, it went back again.”
sentences.n: “schneeweißem Kleide” — in a snow-white garment (dative with old -e). “gehemmt” (hemmen, ‘to stem, hold back’). “zugemacht” (zumachen), “zurückgegangen” (zurückgehen) are separable. “es muß ein Engel gewesen sein” = ‘it must have been an angel.’

sentences.t: Der König sprach „verhält es sich wie du sagst, so will ich diese Nacht bei dir wachen.“
sentences.l: The king spoke, “if it stands as you say, then I will watch this night with you.”
sentences.i: The king said, “If it is as you say, then I will keep watch with you this night.”
sentences.n: “verhält es sich so” — the reflexive sich verhalten means ‘to be the case, stand thus’; the clause with verb first (‘if ...’) is an inverted conditional. “wachen” = to keep watch, stay awake.

sentences.t: Als es dunkel ward, kam der König in den Garten, und brachte einen Priester mit, der sollte den Geist anreden.
sentences.l: When it became dark, the king came into the garden, and brought a priest along, who was to address the spirit.
sentences.i: When it grew dark, the king came into the garden and brought a priest with him, who was to speak to the ghost.
sentences.n: “ward” again — archaic preterite of werden. “brachte ... mit” (mitbringen, ‘to bring along’) is separable. “sollte ... anreden” — sollen expressing intention/task, ‘was to address.’

sentences.t: Alle drei setzten sich unter den Baum und gaben acht.
sentences.l: All three seated themselves under the tree and gave heed.
sentences.i: All three sat down under the tree and kept watch.
sentences.n: “setzten sich” — reflexive sich setzen, ‘to sit down.’ “acht geben” is an idiom, ‘to pay attention, keep watch’ (acht here is a noun, though written lowercase in this edition).

sentences.t: Um Mitternacht kam das Mädchen aus dem Gebüsch gekrochen, trat zu dem Baum, und aß wieder mit dem Munde eine Birne ab; neben ihr aber stand der Engel im weißen Kleide.
sentences.l: At midnight the girl came crawling out of the bushes, stepped to the tree, and again ate a pear off with her mouth; but beside her stood the angel in the white garment.
sentences.i: At midnight the girl came crawling out of the bushes, stepped up to the tree, and again ate a pear off it with her mouth; but beside her stood the angel in the white garment.
sentences.n: “kam ... gekrochen” — the pattern kommen + past participle (kriechen, ‘to crawl’) expresses manner of coming, ‘came crawling.’ “aß ... ab” (abessen) is separable.

sentences.t: Da gieng der Priester hervor und sprach „bist du von Gott gekommen oder von der Welt? bist du ein Geist oder ein Mensch?“
sentences.l: Then the priest went forth and spoke, “are you come from God or from the world? are you a spirit or a human being?”
sentences.i: Then the priest stepped forward and said, “Have you come from God or from the world? Are you a ghost or a mortal?”
sentences.n: “gieng ... hervor” (hervorgehen, ‘to step forth’) is separable. “von der Welt” contrasts with “von Gott” — earthly versus divine. “Mensch” = human being, mortal.

sentences.t: Sie antwortete „ich bin kein Geist, sondern ein armer Mensch, von allen verlassen, nur von Gott nicht.“
sentences.l: She answered, “I am no spirit, but a poor human being, forsaken by all, only not by God.”
sentences.i: She answered, “I am no ghost, but a poor mortal, forsaken by all — only not by God.”
sentences.n: “kein ... sondern” = ‘not a ... but (rather).’ “von allen verlassen” = forsaken by all; the religious keynote returns — abandoned by everyone except God.

sentences.t: Der König sprach „wenn du von aller Welt verlassen bist, so will ich dich nicht verlassen.“
sentences.l: The king spoke, “if you are forsaken by all the world, then I will not forsake you.”
sentences.i: The king said, “If you are forsaken by all the world, then I will not forsake you.”
sentences.n: “von aller Welt” = by all the world, by everyone. The king’s pledge answers her lament almost word for word (verlassen ... verlassen).

sentences.t: Er nahm sie mit sich in sein königliches Schloß, und weil sie so schön und fromm war, liebte er sie von Herzen, ließ ihr silberne Hände machen und nahm sie zu seiner Gemahlin.
sentences.l: He took her with himself into his royal castle, and because she was so beautiful and pious, he loved her from the heart, had silver hands made for her, and took her as his consort.
sentences.i: He took her with him into his royal castle, and because she was so beautiful and pious, he loved her with all his heart, had silver hands made for her, and took her as his wife.
sentences.n: “ließ ihr silberne Hände machen” — lassen + infinitive, ‘had silver hands made for her’ (ihr is dative of the beneficiary). “zu seiner Gemahlin nehmen” = to take as one’s wife/consort. “von Herzen” = from the heart, dearly.

sentences.t: Nach einem Jahre mußte der König über Feld ziehen, da befahl er die junge Königin seiner Mutter, und sprach „wenn sie ins Kindbett kommt, so haltet und verpflegt sie wohl und schreibt mirs gleich in einem Briefe.“
sentences.l: After a year the king had to go over the field, so he commended the young queen to his mother, and spoke, “when she comes into childbed, then keep and tend her well, and write it to me at once in a letter.”
sentences.i: After a year the king had to take the field, so he entrusted the young queen to his mother and said, “When she goes into childbed, keep and tend her well, and write it to me at once in a letter.”
sentences.n: “über Feld ziehen” = to march off, take the field (go to war). “befahl er die Königin seiner Mutter” — here befehlen has its older sense ‘to commend, entrust’ (+ dative of the person entrusted to). “Kindbett” = childbed, confinement. “schreibt mirs” = schreibt mir es. This letter is the hinge of the coming forgery motif.

sentences.t: Nun gebar sie einen schönen Sohn.
sentences.l: Now she bore a beautiful son.
sentences.i: In time she bore a fine son.
sentences.n: “gebar” is the preterite of gebären (‘to bear, give birth to’).

sentences.t: Da schrieb es die alte Mutter eilig und meldete ihm die frohe Nachricht.
sentences.l: Then the old mother wrote it hastily and reported to him the glad news.
sentences.i: Then the old mother wrote it in haste and sent him the glad news.
sentences.n: “meldete” (melden) = to announce, report. “Nachricht” = news, tidings. “frohe” = glad, joyful — the true message, before the Devil’s tampering.

sentences.t: Der Bote aber ruhte unterwegs an einem Bache, und da er von dem langen Wege ermüdet war, schlief er ein.
sentences.l: But the messenger rested on the way by a brook, and because he was wearied from the long road, he fell asleep.
sentences.i: But the messenger rested on the way by a brook, and because he was tired out from the long road, he fell asleep.
sentences.n: “Bache” and “Wege” have the old dative -e. “ermüdet” (ermüden, ‘to tire, weary’). “schlief ... ein” (einschlafen, ‘to fall asleep’) is separable. His sleep gives the Devil his opening.

sentences.t: Da kam der Teufel, welcher der frommen Königin immer zu schaden trachtete, und vertauschte den Brief mit einem andern, darin stand daß die Königin einen Wechselbalg zur Welt gebracht hätte.
sentences.l: Then came the Devil, who always sought to harm the pious queen, and exchanged the letter with another, in which stood that the queen had brought a changeling into the world.
sentences.i: Then came the Devil, who was ever seeking to harm the pious queen, and swapped the letter for another, in which it was written that the queen had brought a changeling into the world.
sentences.n: This is the tale’s forged-letters motif, which now drives the plot: the Devil intercepts the sleeping messenger and swaps the letters. “trachtete zu schaden” (schaden + dative) = ‘sought to harm.’ “vertauschte” (vertauschen) = to exchange, swap. “Wechselbalg” = changeling (a demon child left in a real child’s place). “gebracht hätte” is subjunctive II of reported content.

sentences.t: Als der König den Brief las, erschrack er und betrübte sich sehr, doch schrieb er zur Antwort, sie sollten die Königin wohl halten und pflegen bis zu seiner Ankunft.
sentences.l: When the king read the letter, he took fright and grieved himself greatly, yet he wrote in reply that they should keep and tend the queen well until his arrival.
sentences.i: When the king read the letter, he was alarmed and grieved deeply, yet he wrote back that they should keep and tend the queen well until his return.
sentences.n: “erschrack” is the 1857/archaic preterite of intransitive erschrecken (‘took fright’); modern spelling is erschrak. “betrübte sich” — reflexive sich betrüben, ‘to grieve, be saddened.’ “sollten ... halten und pflegen” is subjunctive of reported command. The king’s good heart shows: even at this news he orders mercy.

sentences.t: Der Bote gieng mit dem Brief zurück, ruhte an der nämlichen Stelle und schlief wieder ein.
sentences.l: The messenger went back with the letter, rested at the selfsame spot, and fell asleep again.
sentences.i: The messenger went back with the letter, rested at the very same spot, and fell asleep again.
sentences.n: “gieng ... zurück” (zurückgehen) and “schlief ... ein” (einschlafen) are separable. “nämlichen” (nämlich as adjective) = ‘same, selfsame.’ The repeated sleep sets up the second forgery.

sentences.t: Da kam der Teufel abermals und legte ihm einen andern Brief in die Tasche, darin stand sie sollten die Königin mit ihrem Kinde tödten.
sentences.l: Then the Devil came once again and laid another letter in his pocket, in which stood that they should kill the queen with her child.
sentences.i: Then the Devil came once more and slipped another letter into his pocket, in which it stood that they should put the queen and her child to death.
sentences.n: “abermals” = once again, once more. “tödten” is the 1857 spelling of töten (‘to kill’), with the old inserted -d-. Now the forgery escalates from slander to a death order.

sentences.t: Die alte Mutter erschrack heftig als sie den Brief erhielt, konnte es nicht glauben und schrieb dem Könige noch einmal, aber sie bekam keine andere Antwort, weil der Teufel dem Boten jedesmal einen falschen Brief unterschob: und in dem letzten Briefe stand noch sie sollten zum Wahrzeichen Zunge und Augen der Königin aufheben.
sentences.l: The old mother took fright violently when she received the letter, could not believe it, and wrote to the king once more, but she got no other answer, because the Devil slipped a false letter to the messenger every time: and in the last letter it further stood that they should keep, as a token, the queen’s tongue and eyes.
sentences.i: The old mother was violently alarmed when she received the letter, could not believe it, and wrote to the king once more; but she got no other answer, for the Devil substituted a false letter for the messenger every time — and the last letter said further that, as a token, they should keep the queen’s tongue and eyes.
sentences.n: “erschrack” again — the old preterite. “Könige / Briefe” have the old dative -e. “unterschob” (unterschieben, ‘to slip in, substitute’). “Wahrzeichen” = token, sign of proof. The grisly proof-token demand (tongue and eyes) is what the old mother will fake with a hind. “aufheben” = to keep, preserve.

sentences.t: Aber die alte Mutter weinte daß so unschuldiges Blut sollte vergossen werden, ließ in der Nacht eine Hirschkuh holen, schnitt ihr Zunge und Augen aus und hob sie auf.
sentences.l: But the old mother wept that such innocent blood should be shed, had a hind fetched in the night, cut out its tongue and eyes, and kept them.
sentences.i: But the old mother wept that such innocent blood should be shed; in the night she had a hind fetched, cut out its tongue and eyes, and kept them.
sentences.n: “vergossen werden” — passive of vergießen (‘to shed, spill’); ‘Blut vergießen’ = to shed blood. “Hirschkuh” = hind, a female deer (Hirsch ‘deer’ + Kuh ‘cow’). “hob ... auf” (aufheben, ‘to keep, save’) is separable. She substitutes the deer’s parts to fake the proof-token.

sentences.t: Dann sprach sie zu der Königin „ich kann dich nicht tödten lassen, wie der König befiehlt, aber länger darfst du nicht hier bleiben: geh mit deinem Kinde in die weite Welt hinein und komm nie wieder zurück.“
sentences.l: Then she spoke to the queen, “I cannot have you killed, as the king commands, but you may no longer stay here: go with your child out into the wide world and never come back again.”
sentences.i: Then she said to the queen, “I cannot have you put to death, as the king commands, but you may stay here no longer: go out with your child into the wide world, and never come back again.”
sentences.n: “tödten lassen” — lassen + infinitive, ‘to have (someone) killed.’ “befiehlt” is 3rd sg. of befehlen (‘to command’). “darfst” (dürfen) = ‘may, are allowed to.’ “in die weite Welt hinein” = out into the wide world — the wandering-exile motif.

sentences.t: Sie band ihr das Kind auf den Rücken, und die arme Frau gieng mit weiniglichen Augen fort.
sentences.l: She bound the child onto her back, and the poor woman went away with tearful eyes.
sentences.i: She bound the child onto her back, and the poor woman went away with weeping eyes.
sentences.n: “band ihr das Kind” — dative ihr, ‘bound the child on her (back).’ Since the queen has (as yet) no hands, the child must be tied on. “weiniglichen” is an archaic word for ‘tearful, weeping’ (related to weinen). “gieng ... fort” (fortgehen) is separable.

sentences.t: Sie kam in einen großen wilden Wald, da setzte sie sich auf ihre Knie und betete zu Gott, und der Engel des Herrn erschien ihr und führte sie zu einem kleinen Haus, daran war ein Schildchen mit den Worten „hier wohnt ein jeder frei.“
sentences.l: She came into a great wild wood; there she set herself on her knees and prayed to God, and the angel of the Lord appeared to her and led her to a little house, on which was a little sign with the words, “here everyone dwells free.”
sentences.i: She came into a great wild forest; there she knelt down and prayed to God, and the angel of the Lord appeared to her and led her to a little house, on which was a small sign with the words, “Here everyone dwells free.”
sentences.n: Again prayer brings the angel. “setzte sich auf ihre Knie” = knelt down. “Schildchen” = a little sign / plaque (diminutive of Schild). “hier wohnt ein jeder frei” — the sign of the forest refuge, repeated near the tale’s end.

sentences.t: Aus dem Häuschen kam eine schneeweiße Jungfrau, die sprach „willkommen, Frau Königin,“ und führte sie hinein.
sentences.l: Out of the little house came a snow-white maiden, who spoke, “welcome, lady queen,” and led her in.
sentences.i: Out of the little house came a snow-white maiden, who said, “Welcome, lady queen,” and led her inside.
sentences.n: “Jungfrau” = maiden, virgin (here the angel in a maiden’s form). “Frau Königin” — a courteous form of address, ‘my lady queen.’ “führte ... hinein” (hineinführen) is separable.

sentences.t: Da band sie ihr den kleinen Knaben von dem Rücken und hielt ihn an ihre Brust, damit er trank, und legte ihn dann auf ein schönes gemachtes Bettchen.
sentences.l: Then she unbound the little boy from her back and held him to her breast, so that he drank, and then laid him on a beautifully made-up little bed.
sentences.i: Then she unbound the little boy from her back and held him to her breast to nurse, and then laid him on a beautifully made-up little bed.
sentences.n: “band ... von dem Rücken” — here binden with von means ‘to unbind, untie from.’ “gemachtes Bettchen” = a made-up little bed (gemacht as an adjective). “damit er trank” = so that he might nurse.

sentences.t: Da sprach die arme Frau „woher weißt du daß ich eine Königin war?“
sentences.l: Then the poor woman spoke, “how do you know that I was a queen?”
sentences.i: Then the poor woman said, “How do you know that I was a queen?”
sentences.n: “woher weißt du” = how / from where do you know.

sentences.t: Die weiße Jungfrau antwortete „ich bin ein Engel, von Gott gesandt, dich und dein Kind zu verpflegen.“
sentences.l: The white maiden answered, “I am an angel, sent by God, to tend you and your child.”
sentences.i: The white maiden answered, “I am an angel, sent by God to care for you and your child.”
sentences.n: “von Gott gesandt” = sent by God (gesandt is the past participle of senden). “verpflegen” = to tend, provide for, care for. The divine guardianship of the heroine is made explicit.

sentences.t: Da blieb sie in dem Hause sieben Jahre, und war wohl verpflegt, und durch Gottes Gnade wegen ihrer Frömmigkeit wuchsen ihr die abgehauenen Hände wieder.
sentences.l: Then she stayed in the house seven years, and was well tended, and through God’s grace, on account of her piety, her chopped-off hands grew again.
sentences.i: Then she stayed in the house seven years, and was well cared for, and through God’s grace, because of her piety, her chopped-off hands grew back again.
sentences.n: “Hause” has the old dative -e. “durch Gottes Gnade wegen ihrer Frömmigkeit” = through God’s grace, on account of her piety — the miracle is framed explicitly as a reward for devoutness. “abgehauenen” (abhauen) = chopped-off. “wuchsen ihr ... wieder” — dative ihr (‘grew back for/on her’); wachsen ‘to grow.’

sentences.t: Der König kam endlich aus dem Felde wieder nach Haus, und sein erstes war daß er seine Frau mit dem Kinde sehen wollte.
sentences.l: The king finally came home again from the field, and his first thing was that he wanted to see his wife with the child.
sentences.i: At last the king came home again from the field, and his first wish was to see his wife and the child.
sentences.n: “aus dem Felde” = from the field (of war). “sein erstes war daß ...” = his first (concern) was that ... — a common idiom.

sentences.t: Da fieng die alte Mutter an zu weinen und sprach „du böser Mann, was hast du mir geschrieben daß ich zwei unschuldige Seelen ums Leben bringen sollte!“ und zeigte ihm die beiden Briefe, die der Böse verfälscht hatte, und sprach weiter „ich habe gethan wie du befohlen hast,“ und wies ihm die Wahrzeichen, Zunge und Augen.
sentences.l: Then the old mother began to weep and spoke, “you wicked man, what have you written to me, that I should bring two innocent souls to death!” and showed him the two letters that the Evil One had falsified, and spoke further, “I have done as you commanded,” and showed him the tokens, tongue and eyes.
sentences.i: Then the old mother began to weep and said, “You wicked man, what have you written to me, that I should put two innocent souls to death!” and she showed him the two letters that the Evil One had forged, and went on, “I have done as you commanded,” and showed him the tokens — the tongue and the eyes.
sentences.n: “fieng ... an” is the 1857 spelling of fing an (anfangen, ‘to begin’), separable. “ums Leben bringen” = to put to death, kill. “verfälscht” (verfälschen) = falsified, forged. “gethan” is the 1857 spelling of getan (past participle of thun/tun). “befohlen” (befehlen) = commanded. “wies ihm” (weisen) = showed him. The mother’s deception is now revealed to the king.

sentences.t: Da fieng der König an noch viel bitterlicher zu weinen über seine arme Frau und sein Söhnlein, daß es die alte Mutter erbarmte, und sie zu ihm sprach „gib dich zufrieden, sie lebt noch. Ich habe eine Hirschkuh heimlich schlachten lassen und von dieser die Wahrzeichen genommen, deiner Frau aber habe ich ihr Kind auf den Rücken gebunden, und sie geheißen in die weite Welt zu gehen, und sie hat versprechen müssen nie wieder hierher zu kommen, weil du so zornig über sie wärst.“
sentences.l: Then the king began to weep much more bitterly still over his poor wife and his little son, so that it moved the old mother to pity, and she spoke to him, “content yourself, she is still alive. I had a hind secretly slaughtered and took the tokens from it, but to your wife I bound her child onto her back and bade her go into the wide world, and she had to promise never to come here again, because you were so angry with her.”
sentences.i: Then the king began to weep much more bitterly over his poor wife and his little son, so that the old mother took pity on him, and said to him, “Be comforted, she is still alive. I had a hind secretly slaughtered and took the tokens from it; but your wife I bound her child onto her back and bade her go into the wide world, and she had to promise never to come back here, because you were so angry with her.”
sentences.n: “bitterlicher” = more bitterly (comparative of bitterlich). “es erbarmte die alte Mutter” — impersonal, ‘it moved the old mother to pity.’ “gib dich zufrieden” = ‘content yourself, be comforted.’ “schlachten lassen” = to have (something) slaughtered. “geheißen” (heißen, here ‘to bid, command’) = ‘bidden.’ “versprechen müssen” — ‘to have had to promise.’ “wärst” is subjunctive II of sein in the reported reason.

sentences.t: Da sprach der König, „ich will gehen so weit der Himmel blau ist, und nicht essen und nicht trinken bis ich meine liebe Frau und mein Kind wieder gefunden habe, wenn sie nicht in der Zeit umgekommen oder Hungers gestorben sind.“
sentences.l: Then the king spoke, “I will go as far as the sky is blue, and not eat and not drink until I have found my dear wife and my child again, unless they have perished in the meantime or died of hunger.”
sentences.i: Then the king said, “I will go as far as the sky is blue, and neither eat nor drink until I have found my dear wife and my child again — unless in that time they have perished or starved to death.”
sentences.n: “so weit der Himmel blau ist” = as far as the sky is blue, i.e. to the ends of the earth. “umgekommen” (umkommen, ‘to perish’). “Hungers gestorben” — an old genitive of cause, ‘died of hunger, starved.’ The king’s vow mirrors the heroine’s earlier fasting piety.

sentences.t: Darauf zog der König umher, an die sieben Jahre lang, und suchte sie in allen Steinklippen und Felsenhöhlen, aber er fand sie nicht und dachte sie wäre verschmachtet.
sentences.l: Thereupon the king wandered about, for close to seven years, and sought her in all rocky cliffs and rock-caves, but he did not find her and thought she had languished away.
sentences.i: After that the king wandered about for nearly seven years, and sought her among all the crags and rock-caves, but he did not find her, and thought she had perished.
sentences.n: “zog ... umher” (umherziehen, ‘to wander about’) is separable. “an die sieben Jahre” = close to / about seven years. “Steinklippen” (stony cliffs, crags) and “Felsenhöhlen” (rock-caves) are compounds. “wäre verschmachtet” is subjunctive of reported thought.

sentences.t: Er aß nicht und trank nicht während dieser ganzen Zeit, aber Gott erhielt ihn.
sentences.l: He did not eat and did not drink during this whole time, but God sustained him.
sentences.i: He neither ate nor drank during all this time, but God sustained him.
sentences.n: “erhielt” here (erhalten) has the older sense ‘to keep alive, sustain, preserve,’ not ‘received.’ God sustains the fasting king just as the angel sustained the fasting queen — a deliberate echo.

sentences.t: Endlich kam er in einen großen Wald und fand darin das kleine Häuschen, daran das Schildchen war mit den Worten „hier wohnt jeder frei.“
sentences.l: Finally he came into a great wood and found in it the little house, on which was the little sign with the words, “here everyone dwells free.”
sentences.i: At last he came into a great forest and found in it the little house, on which was the little sign with the words, “Here everyone dwells free.”
sentences.n: The refuge and its sign return — the same “hier wohnt jeder frei” the queen found seven years earlier, now bringing the king to her.

sentences.t: Da kam die weiße Jungfrau heraus, nahm ihn bei der Hand, führte ihn hinein, und sprach „seid willkommen, Herr König,“ und fragte ihn wo er her käme.
sentences.l: Then the white maiden came out, took him by the hand, led him in, and spoke, “be welcome, lord king,” and asked him where he came from.
sentences.i: Then the white maiden came out, took him by the hand, led him inside, and said, “Be welcome, my lord king,” and asked him where he came from.
sentences.n: “seid willkommen” — the respectful plural imperative of sein, ‘be welcome.’ “wo er her käme” — käme is subjunctive of the reported question (‘where he came from’). She greets him just as she once greeted the queen.

sentences.t: Er antwortete „ich bin bald sieben Jahre umher gezogen, und suche meine Frau mit ihrem Kinde, ich kann sie aber nicht finden.“
sentences.l: He answered, “I have wandered about for nearly seven years, and seek my wife with her child, but I cannot find them.”
sentences.i: He answered, “I have wandered about for nearly seven years, seeking my wife and her child, but I cannot find them.”
sentences.n: “bald sieben Jahre” = nearly seven years. “umher gezogen” (umherziehen) is separable, here in the perfect tense.

sentences.t: Der Engel bot ihm Essen und Trinken an, er nahm es aber nicht, und wollte nur ein wenig ruhen.
sentences.l: The angel offered him food and drink, but he did not take it, and wanted only to rest a little.
sentences.i: The angel offered him food and drink, but he would not take it, and wished only to rest a little.
sentences.n: “bot ... an” (anbieten, ‘to offer’) is separable. Still keeping his vow, the king refuses food — his fast is not yet ended.

sentences.t: Da legte er sich schlafen, und deckte ein Tuch über sein Gesicht.
sentences.l: Then he lay down to sleep, and drew a cloth over his face.
sentences.i: Then he lay down to sleep, and drew a cloth over his face.
sentences.n: “legte sich schlafen” = lay down to sleep. The cloth (Tuch) over the face becomes the pivot of the recognition scene that follows.

sentences.t: Darauf gieng der Engel in die Kammer, wo die Königin mit ihrem Sohne saß, den sie gewöhnlich Schmerzenreich nannte, und sprach zu ihr „geh heraus mit sammt deinem Kinde, dein Gemahl ist gekommen.“
sentences.l: Thereupon the angel went into the chamber, where the queen sat with her son, whom she usually called Sorrowful, and spoke to her, “go out together with your child, your husband has come.”
sentences.i: Then the angel went into the chamber where the queen sat with her son, whom she usually called Sorrowful, and said to her, “Go out, you and your child together, your husband has come.”
sentences.n: “Sohne” has the old dative -e. “Schmerzenreich” is the boy’s name, ‘Rich-in-sorrows’ (Schmerzen ‘sorrows’ + reich ‘rich’) — kept untranslated as a name, its meaning glossed. “mit sammt” (= mitsamt) = together with. “Gemahl” = husband, consort.

sentences.t: Da gieng sie hin wo er lag, und das Tuch fiel ihm vom Angesicht.
sentences.l: Then she went there where he lay, and the cloth fell from his face.
sentences.i: Then she went to where he lay, and the cloth fell from his face.
sentences.n: “gieng ... hin” (hingehen) is separable. “Angesicht” is an elevated word for ‘face, countenance.’ “fiel ihm vom Angesicht” — dative ihm, ‘fell from his face.’

sentences.t: Da sprach sie „Schmerzenreich, heb deinem Vater das Tuch auf und decke ihm sein Gesicht wieder zu.“
sentences.l: Then she spoke, “Sorrowful, pick up the cloth for your father and cover his face again.”
sentences.i: Then she said, “Sorrowful, pick up your father’s cloth and cover his face again.”
sentences.n: “heb ... auf” (aufheben, ‘to pick up’) and “decke ... zu” (zudecken, ‘to cover up’) are separable — the prefixes auf and zu jump to the end. “deinem Vater ... ihm” are datives, ‘for your father ... for him.’

sentences.t: Das Kind hob es auf und deckte es wieder über sein Gesicht.
sentences.l: The child picked it up and covered it again over his face.
sentences.i: The child picked it up and drew it over his face again.
sentences.n: “hob ... auf” (aufheben) is separable, in the preterite.

sentences.t: Das hörte der König im Schlummer und ließ das Tuch noch einmal gerne fallen.
sentences.l: The king heard that in his slumber and let the cloth fall once more, willingly.
sentences.i: The king heard this in his slumber, and gladly let the cloth fall once more.
sentences.n: “Schlummer” = slumber. “ließ ... fallen” — lassen + infinitive, ‘let (it) fall.’ Half-asleep, he lets the cloth drop again to hear the child speak — the recognition builds.

sentences.t: Da ward das Knäbchen ungeduldig und sagte „liebe Mutter, wie kann ich meinem Vater das Gesicht zudecken, ich habe ja keinen Vater auf der Welt? Ich habe das Beten gelernt, unser Vater, der du bist im Himmel; da hast du gesagt mein Vater wär im Himmel und wäre der liebe Gott: wie soll ich einen so wilden Mann kennen? der ist mein Vater nicht.“
sentences.l: Then the little boy became impatient and said, “dear mother, how can I cover my father’s face — I have no father in the world, have I? I have learned the praying, ‘Our Father, who art in heaven’; there you said my father was in heaven and was the dear God: how should I know such a wild man? that is not my father.”
sentences.i: Then the little boy grew impatient and said, “Dear mother, how can I cover my father’s face — I have no father in the world, have I? I have learned my prayer, ‘Our Father, who art in heaven’; you said my father was in heaven and was the dear Lord — how should I know such a wild man? He is not my father.”
sentences.n: “ward” again — archaic preterite of werden. “Knäbchen” = little boy (diminutive of Knabe). “das Beten gelernt” — Beten is the verbal noun ‘praying.’ The child quotes the opening of the Lord’s Prayer (‘Vater unser, der du bist im Himmel’) — the religious frame culminates here: the boy knows only a heavenly Father. “wär / wäre” are subjunctive of reported speech. “kennen” = to know, recognize (a person).

sentences.t: Wie der König das hörte, richtete er sich auf und fragte wer sie wäre.
sentences.l: When the king heard that, he raised himself up and asked who she was.
sentences.i: When the king heard this, he sat up and asked who she was.
sentences.n: “wie” here means ‘when, as’ (temporal). “richtete sich auf” (sich aufrichten, ‘to raise oneself, sit up’) is separable. “wäre” is subjunctive of the reported question.

sentences.t: Da sagte sie „ich bin deine Frau, und das ist dein Sohn Schmerzenreich.“
sentences.l: Then she said, “I am your wife, and this is your son Sorrowful.”
sentences.n: The recognition is spoken plainly; the boy’s name Schmerzenreich (‘Rich-in-sorrows’) sums up all they have suffered.

sentences.t: Und er sah ihre lebendigen Hände und sprach „meine Frau hatte silberne Hände.“
sentences.l: And he saw her living hands and spoke, “my wife had silver hands.”
sentences.i: And he saw her living hands and said, “My wife had silver hands.”
sentences.n: “lebendigen” = living, of living flesh — the point of doubt: his wife had silver hands, but these are real. The miracle of the regrown hands is now the obstacle to belief.

sentences.t: Sie antwortete „die natürlichen Hände hat mir der gnädige Gott wieder wachsen lassen;“ und der Engel gieng in die Kammer, holte die silbernen Hände und zeigte sie ihm.
sentences.l: She answered, “the natural hands the gracious God has let grow again for me;” and the angel went into the chamber, fetched the silver hands, and showed them to him.
sentences.i: She answered, “The gracious God has let my natural hands grow again for me”; and the angel went into the chamber, fetched the silver hands, and showed them to him.
sentences.n: “natürlichen” = natural, of flesh. “hat mir ... wachsen lassen” — lassen + infinitive, ‘has let grow again for me’ (dative mir). “gnädige” = gracious, merciful. The kept silver hands serve as proof that this is indeed his queen.

sentences.t: Da sah er erst gewis daß es seine liebe Frau und sein liebes Kind war, und küßte sie und war froh, und sagte „ein schwerer Stein ist von meinem Herzen gefallen.“
sentences.l: Then he saw for certain that it was his dear wife and his dear child, and kissed them and was glad, and said, “a heavy stone has fallen from my heart.”
sentences.i: Then he knew for certain that it was his dear wife and his dear child, and he kissed them and was glad, and said, “A heavy stone has fallen from my heart.”
sentences.n: “erst gewis” — gewis is the 1857 spelling of gewiss (‘for certain’); erst here = ‘only now, for the first time.’ “ein schwerer Stein ist von meinem Herzen gefallen” is a set idiom, ‘a great weight has been lifted from my heart.’

sentences.t: Da speiste sie der Engel Gottes noch einmal zusammen, und dann giengen sie nach Haus zu seiner alten Mutter.
sentences.l: Then the angel of God fed them once more together, and then they went home to his old mother.
sentences.i: Then the angel of God fed them together once more, and then they went home to his old mother.
sentences.n: “speiste ... zusammen” (zusammenspeisen, ‘to dine / feed together’) is separable. This shared meal ends the king’s long fast. “giengen” is the 1857 spelling of gingen.

sentences.t: Da war große Freude überall, und der König und die Königin hielten noch einmal Hochzeit, und sie lebten vergnügt bis an ihr seliges Ende.
sentences.l: Then there was great joy everywhere, and the king and the queen held their wedding once more, and they lived contentedly until their blessed end.
sentences.i: Then there was great joy everywhere, and the king and queen held their wedding a second time, and they lived contentedly to their blessed end.
sentences.n: “hielten ... Hochzeit” = held a wedding (Hochzeit halten). “vergnügt” = contented, merry. “bis an ihr seliges Ende” = until their blessed end — a pious closing formula, ‘blessed’ (selig) rounding off the tale’s devout frame.
